ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated entry for pan-fried Singaporean tapioca cake; closest parent match is the general tapioca cake food product class (FOODON_03315671).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for pan-fried Singaporean tapioca cake; A0CKP (starchy snack cake) used as closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tapioca cake as a food product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of tapioca cake is approximately 80 g, equivalent to one standard-sized piece as commonly sold at Singaporean hawker stalls and local bakeries.

nutrient_source.title: Tapioca Cake (Pan-fried) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for pan-fried tapioca cake (F11090061). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for pan-fried Singaporean tapioca cake found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 67 (medium) is estimated based on tapioca-based products and cassava-flour snack cakes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (67) × carbohydrate per serving (46.38 g/100g × 80 g serving = 37.1 g) / 100 = 24.9. Input record GI value of 67 retained as consistent with this analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (17.88 g/100g) with moderate saturated fat from coconut milk
hypertension.guidance: Limit portion size to no more than one small piece (40–50 g) on rare occasions, as the high sugar load and saturated fat from coconut milk can independently elevate blood pressure and contribute to arterial stiffness over time; sodium at 31 mg/100g is low and does not drive the concern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate (46.38 g/100g) from tapioca flour combined with high sugar (17.88 g/100g) and medium-high GI (67), producing a glycemic load of ~24.9 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tapioca cake is not recommended for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its high glycemic load per serving (~24.9); if consumed, restrict to a very small portion (≤30 g) paired with a protein or fat source to blunt the postprandial glucose spike, and monitor blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 5.8 g/100g from coconut milk, combined with high sugar (17.88 g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content (~5.8 g/100g) from coconut milk and the high sugar load make tapioca cake a food to limit for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease; occasional small portions (≤50 g) are acceptable within a diet otherwise low in saturated fat and added sugars.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~5.8 g/100g from coconut milk (exceeds 5 g/100g RA threshold) and high sugar 17.88 g/100g (exceeds 15 g/100g RA threshold); coconut milk is explicitly excluded from the MUFA/PUFA anti-inflammatory carve-out
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tapioca cake is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the saturated fat from coconut milk triggers pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6) and the high sugar content further promotes systemic inflammation — avoid or strictly limit consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tapioca cake (kuih ubi kayu) is a traditional Singaporean and Southeast Asian snack made primarily from tapioca flour (cassava starch), coconut milk, sugar, and eggs, typically flavoured with pandan. The batter is steamed or baked and then pan-fried to produce a soft, slightly chewy interior with a lightly golden, crisp exterior. The texture is dense and moist, with a mildly sweet coconut flavour and a subtle earthy note from the tapioca. It is commonly sold at Singaporean hawker centres, wet markets, and Peranakan kueh stalls. Per 100 g, tapioca cake provides 252 kcal, 1.13 g protein, 6.91 g fat (predominantly saturated fat ~5.8 g from coconut milk), 46.38 g carbohydrate, 17.88 g sugar, 2.55 g dietary fibre, and 31 mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 67 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of ~24.9 per standard 80 g serving, making it a high-glycemic-load food per serving. Clinically, tapioca cake is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high refined carbohydrate and sugar content; it should be limited for those with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis due to its saturated fat and sugar profile. It is gluten-free and halal-certified in most commercial preparations.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tapioca cake?
Is tapioca cake suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of tapioca cake?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11090061) with confidence 0.95; this is the canonical primary source for Singaporean food composition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database consistent with the input record value of 67. Saturated fat estimated from coconut milk composition as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for pan-fried tapioca cake are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F11090061 as of 2026-04-16; the HPB Foodo database was consulted and confirmed these fields are not available for this entry. Saturated fat value of ~5.8 g/100g is estimated from the known fatty acid profile of coconut milk (the primary fat source) and is consistent with the total fat of 6.91 g/100g. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for tapioca-based snack products; the input record value of 67 is retained as consistent with this estimate. Glycemic load per serving recalculated as 67 × (46.38 × 0.80) / 100 = 24.9 (using 80 g serving size). No zero-value core macros are present in this record. Ontology match types set to 'closest_parent' for FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact codes exist for pan-fried Singaporean tapioca cake in either system.
